title: Mapping shapes
description: Describe sequences of rigid transformations (translations, rotations, and/or reflections) that will map a given shape onto another.
topic_path: Topic B: Sequencing the basic rigid motions

assessment_items.question_content:
Triangles $\triangle XYZ$ and $\triangle ABC$ are congruent. The side length of each square on the grid is $1$ unit.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following sequences of transformations maps $\triangle XYZ$ onto $\triangle ABC$?**

Sequence A | Sequence B
:- | :-
A translation $2$ units to the right and $10$ units up, then a reflection over the line $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$. | A translation $6$ units to the right and $6$ units up, then a $180\degree$ rotation about point $B$.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $\triangle XYZ$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence A we perform a translation $2$ units to the right and $10$ units up...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a reflection over the line $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $\triangle XYZ$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence B we perform a translation $6$ units to the right and $6$ units up...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a $180\degree$ rotation about point $B$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $\triangle XYZ$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In conclusion, only sequence B maps $\triangle XYZ$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

*Note:* There are many other possible combinations of transformations that would also map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Triangle A B C has point A at ten, ten, point B at eight, eight, and Point C at two, ten. Triangle X Y Z has point X at the origin, point Y at two, two, and point Z at eight, zero.

assessment_items.question_content:
Triangles $\triangle DEF$ and $\triangle ABC$ are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following sequences of transformations maps $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle ABC$?**

Sequence A | Sequence B
:- | :-
A translation along the directed line segment $\overline{DB}$. then a $90\degree$ rotation about point $B$. | A $90\degree$ rotation about point $F$, then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{FC}$.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence A we perform a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{DB}$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a $90\degree$ rotation about point $B$ (recall that a rotation by a positive angle is counterclockwise).



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence B we perform a $90\degree$ rotation about point $F$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{FC}$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In conclusion, neither of the sequences maps $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

*Note:* There are many possible combinations of transformations that would map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Triangle A B C has point A at seven, ten, point B at zero, ten, and Point C at two, six. Triangle D E F has point D at seven, seven, point E at seven, zero, and point F at eleven, two.

assessment_items.question_content:
Quadrilaterals $LMNO$ and $ABCD$ are congruent. The side length of each square on the grid is $1$ unit.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following sequences of transformations maps $LMNO$ onto $ABCD$?**

Sequence A | Sequence B
:- | :-
A translation $6$ units to the left and $1$ unit up, then a reflection over the horizontal line through point $A$. | A $90\degree$ rotation about point $O$, then a translation $6$ units to the left and $1$ unit up.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $LMNO$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence A we perform a translation $6$ units to the left and $1$ unit up...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a reflection over the horizontal line through point $A$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $LMNO$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence B we perform a $90\degree$ rotation about point $O$ (recall that a rotation by a positive angle is counterclockwise)...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation $6$ units to the left and $1$ unit up.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $LMNO$ onto $ABCD$.

In conclusion, neither of the sequences maps $LMNO$ onto $ABCD$.

*Note:* There are many possible combinations of transformations that would map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Quadrilateral A B C D has point A at five, ten, point B at five, twelve, Point C at six, thirteen, and Point D at nine, thirteen. Quadrilateral L M N O has point L at fourteen, five, point M at twelve, five, point N at eleven, six, and point O at eleven, nine.

assessment_items.question_content:
Quadrilaterals $STUV$ and $ABCD$ are congruent. The side length of each square on the grid is $1$ unit.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following sequences of transformations maps $STUV$ onto $ABCD$?**

Sequence A | Sequence B
:- | :-
A translation $7$ units to the left and $2$ units up, then a $180\degree$ rotation about point $C$. | A $180\degree$ rotation about point $T$, then a translation $3$ units to the right and $8$ units up.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $STUV$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence A we perform a translation $7$ units to the left and $2$ units up...


[[☃ image 2]]

...then a $180\degree$ rotation about point $C$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $STUV$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence B we perform a $180\degree$ rotation about point $T$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation $3$ units to the right and $8$ unit up.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $STUV$ onto $ABCD$.

In conclusion, both sequences map $STUV$ onto $ABCD$.

*Note:* There are many other possible combinations of transformations that would also map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Quadrilateral A B C D has point A at five, fourteen, point B at eleven, thirteen, Point C at six, ten, and Point D at four, eleven. Quadrilateral S T U V has point S at fourteen, four, point T at eight, five, point U at thirteen, eight, and point V at fifteen, seven.

assessment_items.question_content:
Quadrilaterals $EFGH$ and $ABCD$ are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following sequences of transformations maps $EFGH$ onto $ABCD$?**

Sequence A | Sequence B
:- | :-
A $-90\degree$ rotation about point $H$, then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{HD}$. | A $-90\degree$ rotation about point $F$, then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{FA}$.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $EFGH$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence A we perform a $-90\degree$ rotation about point $H$ (recall that a rotation by a negative angle is clockwise)...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{HD}$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $EFGH$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence B we perform a a $-90\degree$ rotation about point $F$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{FA}$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $EFGH$ onto $ABCD$.

In conclusion, only sequence A maps $EFGH$ onto $ABCD$.

*Note:* There are many other possible combinations of transformations that would also map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Quadrilateral A B C D has point A at fourteen, nine, point B at fourteen, six, Point C at eleven, two, and Point D at eleven, five. Quadrilateral E F G H has point E at two, thirteen, point F at five, thirteen, point G at nine, ten, and point H at six, ten.

In Sequence B we perform a a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{UA}$...

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Triangle A B C has point A at five, seven, point B at five, three, and Point C at two, three. Triangle U V W has point U at eleven, eight, point V at eleven, twelve, and point W at fourteen, twelve.

assessment_items.question_content:
Triangles $\triangle KLM$ and $\triangle ABC$ are congruent. The side length of each square on the grid is $1$ unit.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following sequences of transformations maps $\triangle KLM$ onto $\triangle ABC$?**

Sequence A | Sequence B
:- | :-
A translation $9$ units to the left and $7$ units down, then a reflection over the line with slope $-1$ through point $C$. | A  reflection over the line $\overleftrightarrow{KL}$, then a translation $3$ units to the left and $1$ unit down.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $\triangle KLM$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence A we perform a translation $9$ units to the left and $7$ units down...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a reflection over the line with slope $-1$ through point $C$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $\triangle KLM$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence B we perform a reflection over the line $\overleftrightarrow{KL}$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation $3$ units to the left and $1$ unit down.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $\triangle KLM$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In conclusion, both sequences map $\triangle KLM$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

*Note:* There are many other possible combinations of transformations that would also map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Triangle A B C has point A at eight, eight, point B at ten, six, and Point C at four, six. Triangle K L M has point K at eleven, nine, point L at thirteen, seven, and point M at thirteen, thirteen.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $\triangle PQR$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence A we perform a $180\degree$ rotation about point $R$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation $2$ units to the right and $6$ units up.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $\triangle PQR$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence B we perform a translation $4$ units to the right and $6$ units up...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a reflection over the horizontal line through point $A$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $\triangle PQR$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In conclusion, neither of the sequences maps $\triangle PQR$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

*Note:* There are many possible combinations of transformations that would map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Triangle A B C has point A at nine, fourteen, point B at twelve, ten, and Point C at eight, ten. Triangle P Q R has point P at five, eight, point Q at two, four, and point R at six, four.

assessment_items.question_content:

**Which of the following sequences of transformations maps $\triangle MNO$ onto $\triangle ABC$?**

:- | :-
A translation along the directed line segment $\overline{NB}$, then a reflection over the vertical line through point $B$. | A reflection over the vertical line $\overleftrightarrow{MO}$, then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{MB}$.

hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $\triangle MNO$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence A we perform a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{NB}$...

...then a reflection over the vertical line through point $B$.


[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $\triangle MNO$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In Sequence B we perform a reflection over the vertical line $\overleftrightarrow{MO}$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{MB}$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $\triangle MNO$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

In conclusion, only sequence A maps $\triangle MNO$ onto $\triangle ABC$.

*Note:* There are many other possible combinations of transformations that would also map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Triangle A B C has point A at two, fourteen, point B at five, fourteen, and Point C at two, ten. Triangle M N O has point M at thirteen, nine, point N at ten, nine, and point O at thirteen, five.

:- | :-
A $270\degree$ rotation about point $J$, then a translation $9$ units to the right and $8$ units down. | A reflection over the line $\overleftrightarrow{HI}$, then a translation $6$ units to the right and $3$ units down.

hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $HIJK$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence A we perform a $270\degree$ rotation about point $J$ (recall that a rotation by a positive angle is counterclockwise)...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation $9$ units to the right and $8$ units down.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $HIJK$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence B we perform a reflection over the line $\overleftrightarrow{HI}$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a translation $6$ units to the right and $3$ units down.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $HIJK$ onto $ABCD$.

In conclusion, only sequence B maps $HIJK$ onto $ABCD$.

*Note:* There are many other possible combinations of transformations that would also map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Quadrilateral A B C D has point A at nine, four, point B at eleven, six, Point C at fourteen, six, and Point D at fourteen, four. Quadrilateral H I J K has point H at three, seven, point I at five, nine, point J at five, twelve, and point K at three, twelve.

assessment_items.question_content:
Quadrilaterals $ABCD$ and $WXYZ$ are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following sequences of transformations maps $WXYZ$ onto $ABCD$?**

Sequence A | Sequence B
:- | :-
A reflection over the vertical line $\overleftrightarrow{WX}$, then a $180\degree$ rotation about point $W$, and then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{WA}$. | A translation over the directed line segment $\overline{WA}$, then a reflection over the horizontal line through point $A$.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's perform each of the sequences and see if they map $WXYZ$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence A we perform a reflection over the vertical line $\overleftrightarrow{WX}$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a $180\degree$ rotation about point $W$...



[[☃ image 2]]

...then a translation along the directed line segment $\overline{WA}$.



[[☃ image 3]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $WXYZ$ onto $ABCD$.

In Sequence B we perform a translation over the directed line segment $\overline{WA}$...



[[☃ image 1]]

...then a reflection over the horizontal line through point $A$.



[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence *does* map $WXYZ$ onto $ABCD$.

In conclusion, both sequences map $WXYZ$ onto $ABCD$.

*Note:* There are many other possible combinations of transformations that would also map the figure onto the image.

options.alt: The first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. Quadrilateral A B C D has point A at fourteen, eight, point B at fourteen, eleven, Point C at twelve, thirteen, and Point D at ten, thirteen. Quadrilateral W X Y Z has point W at eight, nine, point X at eight, six, point Y at six, four, and point Z at four, four.

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $KLMN$ onto $ABCD$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

We can see that this sequence does *not* map $KLMN$ onto $ABCD$.

In conclusion, neither of the sequences maps $KLMN$ onto $ABCD$.

*Note:* There are many possible combinations of transformations that would map the figure onto the image.

Quadrilateral A B C D has point A at four, nine, point B at seven, seven, Point C at four, three, and Point D at two, seven.
